pi_items.source: Task description specifies save filename. setup_task.ps1 removes this file to ensure clean start. export_result.ps1 checks for this exact filename.
Task description specifies full save path. setup_task.ps1 creates directory and removes pre-existing file at this path.
Task description specifies 4 pages: Page 1 (Title), Page 2 (Food Web), Page 3 (Energy Pyramid), Page 4 (Carbon Cycle).

All organisms are real species found in Pacific Ocean marine ecosystems. Phytoplankton and kelp are primary producers; zooplankton, krill, and sea urchins are primary consumers; herring, sea otters, and orcas are secondary/apex predators. Verified against NOAA marine ecosystem documentation.
Phytoplankton are microscopic photosynthetic organisms forming the base of marine food webs. Kelp (brown algae) are large marine producers. Both confirmed as primary producers in Pacific ecosystems.

Zooplankton feed on phytoplankton. Krill (Euphausiacea) are filter-feeding crustaceans eating phytoplankton and zooplankton. Sea urchins are herbivorous grazers on kelp. All confirmed as primary consumers.

Herring (Clupea pallasii) feed on zooplankton and krill. Sea otters are keystone predators feeding on sea urchins. Orcas (Orcinus orca) are apex predators in the Pacific. All confirmed as secondary or top consumers.

With 8 organisms across 3 trophic levels, ecologically accurate connections include: Phytoplankton->Zooplankton, Phytoplankton->Krill, Kelp->Sea Urchin, Kelp->Krill, Zooplankton->Herring, Zooplankton->Krill, Krill->Herring, Krill->Sea Otter, Sea Urchin->Sea Otter, Herring->Orca, Sea Otter->Orca, Phytoplankton->Herring. At least 12 valid trophic connections exist.
Standard ecological energy pyramid labels for 3-tier marine food web. Energy decreases at each trophic level per the 10% rule.

Standard marine carbon cycle stages. CO2 from atmosphere is absorbed via photosynthesis by marine plants, transferred to marine animals through consumption, released via decomposition, sequestered in ocean sediments, and eventually returned to the atmosphere. Verified against NOAA Ocean Carbon Cycle documentation.

privileged_info_summary: This task creates a 4-page ActivInspire flipchart about a marine ecosystem. Page 1 is a title page. Page 2 features a food web diagram with 8 real Pacific Ocean organisms (Phytoplankton, Kelp, Zooplankton, Krill, Sea Urchin, Herring, Sea Otter, Orca) arranged across 3 trophic levels and connected by at least 12 arrows showing feeding relationships. Page 3 has a 3-tier energy pyramid labeled Producers, Primary Consumers, and Top Predators. Page 4 shows the marine carbon cycle with 6 stages (Atmosphere, Photosynthesis, Marine Plants, Marine Animals, Decomposition, Ocean Sediments) arranged in a loop. All organisms and ecological relationships are scientifically accurate for Pacific Ocean ecosystems. The file is saved as marine_ecosystem.flipchart in C:\Users\Docker\Documents\Flipcharts\.
